units.title: Basics 1
units.description: Your very first French words and sentences. Learn people, food, drinks, and how to say what you are and what you eat.
units.guidebook:
## Welcome to French! 🇫🇷

French nouns have a **gender**: they are either **masculine** (*le*) or **feminine** (*la*). You'll learn the article with each noun, so always remember it!

### The verb *être* (to be)
This is one of the most important verbs. Here are the basics:
- **je suis** — I am
- **tu es** — you are (informal)
- **il est** — he is / **elle est** — she is
- **nous sommes** — we are

### Saying what you eat and drink
French uses **manger** (to eat) and **boire** (to drink):
- *Je mange une pomme.* — I eat an apple.
- *Je bois de l'eau.* — I drink water.

Notice **de l'eau** instead of *la eau*: after *de*, the article *le/la* becomes **de l'** before a vowel.

### A small but vital word
**Oui** (yes) and **non** (no) will be your best friends in every conversation. Use them often!

units.title: Greetings
units.description: Learn essential French greetings and polite expressions for everyday conversations.
units.guidebook:
## Salutations et Politesse 🇫🇷

French greetings vary by time of day and formality. **Bonjour** (good day/hello) is the safe default any time before evening. After dark, switch to **Bonsoir** (good evening). For farewells, **Au revoir** works universally, while **Salut** is casual among friends.

**Tu vs. Vous**: Use **tu** with friends, family, and children. Use **vous** with strangers, elders, and in formal settings. When in doubt, default to **vous** — it's considered more polite.

**Comment ça va ?** (How are you?) is a casual greeting. Reply with **Ça va bien** (I'm doing well) or **Comme ci, comme ça** (so-so).

**Merci** (thank you) and **s'il vous plaît** (please) are essential. To be extra polite, say **Merci beaucoup** (thank you very much). **De rien** (you're welcome) is the standard reply.

**Bonne nuit** (good night) is used when parting for sleep, not as a greeting.

exercises.answer: I am doing well, thank you.

units.title: Food
units.description: Learn essential French words for food and drink, and how to say what you want, eat, and drink.
units.guidebook:
Welcome to **Food**! 🥐

In French, every noun has a **gender**. Masculine nouns use *le* (or *un*), feminine nouns use *la* (or *une*). For example, *le café* (the coffee) is masculine, but *la pizza* is feminine.

To say what you want, use **je veux** (I want): *Je veux du café* — I want (some) coffee. **Du** is the partitive article, used for unspecified quantities of food and drink.

To say what you eat or drink, use **je mange** (I eat) and **je bois** (I drink): *Je mange du riz* (I eat rice), *Je bois du jus* (I drink juice).

**Boire** is irregular: *je bois, tu bois, il boit*. **Manger** is regular but adds *-e* before silent endings: *je mange, tu manges*. Bon appétit! 🍽️

units.title: Animals
units.description: Learn the names of common animals in French and how to describe them simply.
units.guidebook:
## Animals in French 🐾

Welcome! In this unit, you'll learn the names of common animals and how to describe them with simple sentences.

### Gender of animal nouns
Most animal nouns have a fixed gender. For example, **le chien** (the dog) is masculine, while **la chatte** (the female cat) is feminine. The general word **le chat** usually refers to cats in general.

### Using adjectives
French adjectives must agree in **gender and number** with the noun. Add **-e** for feminine (e.g., *grand → grande*) and **-s** for plural (e.g., *petit → petits*). Example: *un petit chien* → *une petite chienne*.

### The verb "être" (to be)
Use **être** to describe animals: *Le chien est grand* (The dog is big). Conjugation: *je suis, tu es, il/elle est, nous sommes, vous êtes, ils/elles sont*.

### Tip
When you learn an animal, also learn its article (**le** or **la**) — it helps you remember the gender naturally!

exercises.answer: The fish is in the water.

units.title: Travel
units.description: Learn essential French vocabulary and phrases for getting around cities, using transportation, staying at hotels, and asking for directions.
units.guidebook:
## Getting Around in French

**Asking where things are** uses the structure: *Où est... ?* (Where is...?) for singular nouns and *Où sont... ?* for plural. To ask for a specific place, use *Où est la gare ?* (Where is the train station?).

**Giving directions** often starts with *Allez tout droit* (Go straight), *Tournez à gauche/droite* (Turn left/right), or *C'est à côté de...* (It's next to...).

**At the airport or train station**, you'll hear *Le train part à...* (The train leaves at...) and *Le vol est retardé* (The flight is delayed). To buy a ticket, ask *Un billet, s'il vous plaît* (One ticket, please).

**Gender matters!** French nouns are masculine or feminine: *le musée* (m.), *la gare* (f.), *l'hôtel* (m.), *la rue* (f.). Always learn the article with the noun.

**Polite phrases** like *Excusez-moi* and *Merci beaucoup* go a long way when navigating a French-speaking city!

exercises.answer: I am looking for the hotel.

exercises.prompt: C'est à côté de la banque.
exercises.audioTarget: C'est à côté de la banque.

exercises.prompt: C'est à côté de la banque.
exercises.audioTarget: C'est à côté de la banque.
exercises.answer: It is next to the bank.

exercises.prompt: Le train part à dix heures.
exercises.audioTarget: Le train part à dix heures.

exercises.prompt: Le train part à dix heures.
exercises.audioTarget: Le train part à dix heures.
exercises.answer: The train leaves at ten o'clock.
